text: This chapter examines the historical evolution of central-local government relations in Japan through the lenses of administration, finance, and politics. By employing a conceptual framework of "interfusion" and "separation," it clarifies the distinct characteristics defining the Japanese intergovernmental landscape. During its modernization phase, Japan looked toward European models and ultimately adopted an interfusion system, in which the central government delegates policy implementation to local authorities to ensure national standards. Throughout the 20th century, this relationship shifted through phases of wartime functional centralization, postwar decentralization under the Allied Occupation, and the more recent decentralization reforms initiated in the 1990s. Despite these shifts, the fundamental quality of interfusion has been preserved and even bolstered by large-scale municipal mergers and sophisticated fiscal adjustment mechanisms. Consequently, while Japan’s local governments account for a exceptionally high proportion of total public activity by international standards, this scale is fundamentally sustained by an integrated, interfusion-type relationship with the central state.
